title: TAKE IT OFF
speaker: Pastor Ashley Evans
keyVerseText: You were taught, with regard to your former way of life, to put off your old self, which is being corrupted by its deceitful desires; to be made new in the attitude of your minds; and to put on the new self, created to be like God in true righteousness and holiness.

content.value: Identity is visible. It moves into the room before your mouth opens.
The clothes are not matching the calling.
The Holy Spirit's instruction was not about fashion — it was about:

content.before: Wearing the clothes of who you
Not the clothes of who you

content.value: You can choose the right power and still walk into the room wearing the wrong self.
Right theology + wrong identity = broken delivery
The fuel can be perfect but the delivery system is broken
You cannot carry the right power in the wrong identity.
Paul gives us a complete wardrobe transaction. Three moves:
Move 1: Put Off
Greek: apothesthai — to strip off completely
The old self is being corrupted — present tense, active decay
You cannot renovate something that is decomposing
You don't patch rot. You strip it out.

content.before: Covered up is not the same as

content.value: Move 2: Be Made New
Greek: passive voice — something that happens TO you
The old self was built by voices, wounds, and narratives you didn't write
You cannot think your way out of something installed deeper than thought
Move 3: Put On
Greek: endusasthai — to clothe yourself fully
The new self is not a better version of the old self
It is a completely different creation — created to be like God
Wrong self. Wrong outcome.  |  New self. Exceedingly. Abundantly. Above.
Paul's old self was not a mess. It was the most impressive résumé in the room:
Circumcised on the eighth day. Tribe of Benjamin. A Hebrew of Hebrews.
A Pharisee. Student of Gamaliel. As for righteousness based on the law — faultless.

content.text: I consider everything a loss because of the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord. For his sake I have suffered the loss of all things and count them as rubbish, in order that I may gain Christ.

content.value: Skubalon (Greek) — used once in the entire New Testament. Means: dung. Refuse. Waste.

content.before: The old self God asks you to take off might be your most
It's not what God made for you — it's what you made for

content.text: As they were walking along and talking together, suddenly a chariot of fire and horses of fire appeared and separated the two of them, and Elijah went up to heaven in a whirlwind.
Elisha saw this and cried out, 'My father! My father! The chariots and horsemen of Israel!' And Elisha saw him no more. Then he took hold of his garment and tore it in two.
Elisha then picked up Elijah's cloak that had fallen from him and went back and stood on the bank of the Jordan.

Then he took the cloak of Elijah that had fallen from him and struck the water with it. 'Where is the Lord, the God of Elijah?' he asked. When he struck the water, it divided to the right and to the left, and he crossed over.

content.value: Before Elisha picked up the mantle — he tore off his own clothes.
You cannot pick up what God is dropping while you are still wearing what you arrived in.
The servant's clothes could not hold the prophet's mantle
He stripped them — not because they were bad, but because they were finished
The river responds to what you're wearing, not what you're holding

sections.title: What the Early Church Did — Baptism

content.value: The Easter Baptism Vigil (2nd–4th Century)
Baptism happened once a year — only on Easter, before sunrise
Candidates faced west and renounced Satan out loud

content.text: I renounce thee, Satan, and all thy works, and all thy pomps.

content.value: Then turned east toward the risen Christ and declared their faith
Stripped of everything before entering the water — nothing carried across
Cyril of Jerusalem: an imitation of Christ's nakedness on the cross
The White Robe
Came up from the water — someone was waiting with a pure white robe

content.text: The servant of God is clothed with the robe of righteousness.

content.value: In the Eastern church they wore the white robe for the full eight days of Easter
Coming to Christ is not behaviour modification. It is a wardrobe change.
We are two weeks from Easter. We are doing baptisms. Baptism is not a graduation ceremony. It is for people who are ready to take it off.
Picture yourself at the Jordan River. Old clothes on.

Take off the shrunken self → Put on the self that stands with shoulders back
Take off the wounded self → Put on the self rooted and established in love
Take off the self waiting for permission → Put on the self God commissioned
Take off the afraid self → Put on the self that walks to the Jordan and shouts:

content.text: Where is the God of Elijah?

content.value: He is still here. He is the same God.
He took it off so you could put it on. That is the gospel in one sentence.

responsePrompts: What 'old self' is God asking me to take off?
What is the 'new self' God has prepared for me?
commitments: I'm ready to take off the old self today
